title: Verb Group 3: Movement & Life Management — Translation

exercises.question: What is the Portuguese verb for 'to work'?
exercises.explanation: 'Trabalhar' is the regular -ar verb for work: 'Onde você trabalha?' (Where do you work?). 'Morar' means to reside, 'sair' to leave, 'ajudar' to help — none describe professional activity.

exercises.question: What is the Portuguese verb for 'to live' or 'to reside' somewhere?
exercises.explanation: 'Morar' expresses where you live: 'Moro em São Paulo.' 'Trabalhar' means to work, 'chegar' to arrive, 'trazer' to bring — none mean permanent residence.

exercises.question: What is the Portuguese verb for 'to leave' or 'to go out'?
exercises.explanation: 'Sair' covers leaving and going out: 'Vou sair agora.' 'Chegar' is the opposite — to arrive. 'Morar' means to reside, 'trabalhar' to work.

exercises.question: What is the Portuguese verb for 'to arrive'?
exercises.explanation: 'Chegar' means to arrive: 'Que horas você chega?' 'Sair' is the opposite — to leave. 'Trazer' and 'levar' describe moving objects, not a person arriving somewhere.

exercises.question: What is the Portuguese verb for 'to wait'?
exercises.explanation: 'Esperar' means to wait: 'Espera um momento!' 'Ver' means to see, 'pedir' to request something, 'ajudar' to help — none express waiting.

exercises.question: What is the Portuguese verb for 'to see' or 'to watch' (as in watching TV)?
exercises.explanation: 'Ver televisão' is the standard phrase for watching TV. 'Olhar' is intentional directed looking, not passive viewing. 'Trazer' means to bring, 'ligar' to call or switch on.

exercises.question: What is the Portuguese verb for 'to look at' — intentionally directing your gaze?
exercises.explanation: 'Olhar para' expresses directed, intentional looking: 'Olha para mim!' 'Ver' is passive perception. 'Abrir' means to open, 'pedir' to ask for something.

exercises.question: What is the Portuguese verb for 'to put' or 'to place' an object somewhere?
exercises.explanation: 'Colocar' means to put or place: 'Coloca o livro na mesa.' 'Levar' moves things away from here, 'trazer' brings things here, 'mandar' sends remotely — none mean simply placing an object.

exercises.question: What is the Portuguese verb for 'to take' an object away from your location?
exercises.explanation: 'Levar' moves objects away from the speaker: 'Leva o guarda-chuva.' 'Trazer' is the opposite — bring here. 'Colocar' places an object without moving it far, 'mandar' sends remotely without physical movement.

exercises.question: What is the Portuguese verb for 'to bring' an object to your location?
exercises.explanation: 'Trazer' moves objects toward the speaker: 'Traz o guarda-chuva!' 'Levar' is the opposite — take away. 'Colocar' places an object, 'mandar' sends remotely.

exercises.question: What is the Portuguese verb for 'to open'?
exercises.explanation: 'Abrir' means to open: 'Abre a porta, por favor.' 'Fechar' is the opposite — to close. 'Ligar' means to call or turn on, 'colocar' to place something.

exercises.question: What is the Portuguese verb for 'to close'?
exercises.explanation: 'Fechar' means to close: 'Fecha a janela.' 'Abrir' is the opposite — to open. 'Colocar' means to place, 'ligar' to call or turn on.

exercises.question: What is the Portuguese verb for 'to call' someone by phone?
exercises.explanation: 'Ligar para alguém' means to phone someone: 'Vou te ligar mais tarde.' 'Mandar' means to send a message, 'perguntar' to ask a question, 'pedir' to request something — none mean making a phone call.

exercises.question: What is the Portuguese verb for 'to turn on' a light or electrical device?
exercises.explanation: 'Ligar' covers both 'call' and 'turn on': 'Liga a televisão.' 'Fechar' means to close, 'abrir' to open, 'mandar' to send — none mean switching something on.

exercises.question: What is the Portuguese verb for 'to send' a message, file, or item?
exercises.explanation: 'Mandar' means to send: 'Manda uma mensagem.' 'Levar' requires physical movement by the sender, 'pedir' requests something, 'perguntar' asks a question — none mean dispatching or transmitting.

exercises.question: What is the Portuguese verb for 'to order' food or 'to ask for' a favor?
exercises.explanation: 'Pedir' covers ordering food and requesting favors: 'Pede a conta.' 'Perguntar' is for informational questions, not ordering. 'Ajudar' means to help, 'mandar' to send.

exercises.question: What is the Portuguese verb for 'to ask' a question to get information?
exercises.explanation: 'Perguntar' is for seeking information: 'Posso te perguntar uma coisa?' 'Pedir' requests objects or favors, not information. 'Mandar' means to send, 'ajudar' to help.

exercises.question: What is the Portuguese verb for 'to help' someone?
exercises.explanation: 'Ajudar' means to help: 'Pode me ajudar?' 'Pedir' means to ask for something, 'perguntar' to ask a question, 'trazer' to bring an object — none mean offering assistance.

exercises.question: Which verb from this group also means 'to hope' in addition to its main meaning of 'to wait'?
exercises.explanation: 'Esperar' uniquely covers both meanings: 'Espero que dê certo!' (I hope it works out!). 'Olhar' means to look at, 'ver' to see, 'ajudar' to help — none can express hope.

exercises.question: Which verb from this group covers two distinct meanings: 'to call by phone' AND 'to turn on a device'?
exercises.explanation: 'Ligar' is uniquely dual-purpose: 'Vou te ligar' (I'll call you) and 'Liga a luz' (Turn on the light). 'Mandar' means to send, 'abrir' to open, 'pedir' to request — none share this dual role.
